title: Orobanche mayeri
intro: Orobanche mayeri, commonly known as the bee orchid broomrape or Mayer's broomrape, is a parasitic plant species that belongs to the Orobanchaceae family. Traditionally, Orobanche mayeri has been used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, due to its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Orobanche mayeri include alkaloids, phenylethanoids, and phenylpropanoids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In traditional herbal medicine, Orobanche mayeri is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and it can be found in various herbal supplements and preparations, including teas, tinctures, and capsules.
taxonomy: Orobanche mayeri is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Orobanchaceae, commonly known as the broomrape family. This family is part of the order Lamiales, a group of flowering plants that includes mint, basil, and snapdragons. The Orobanchaceae family is characterized by its parasitic nature, where plants obtain nutrients from other plants rather than through photosynthesis. Orobanche mayeri is a parasitic plant that grows on the roots of other plants, specifically on those of the genera Allium and Aconitum. It is native to the mountainous regions of Central and Eastern Europe.

side_effects: Orobanche mayeri is a parasitic plant that can have severe side effects when ingested or handled improperly. Its toxins can cause irritation, inflammation, and even allergic reactions in some individuals. Ingesting Orobanche mayeri can lead to gastrointestinal issues, nausea, and vomiting.
